1.blurb: By 1300 the Mongol Empire had broken into four khanates: the Yuan in China, the Ilkhanate in Iran, the Chagatai in Central Asia, and the Golden Horde on the Russian steppe. The Ilkhanate would collapse in 1335 and the Yuan in 1368 — both during the European late medieval period. The same Little Ice Age cooling that produced European famine appears to have stressed Mongol pastoralism on the steppe and contributed to the political instability that broke the empire.

The Yuan dynasty — the Mongol regime in China — was founded by Kublai Khan after his conquest of the Southern Song in 1279. The fall of the Yuan to the Ming founder Zhu Yuanzhang in 1368 happened mid-Hundred-Years War and is one of the major Eurasian events overshadowed by the European-centric textbooks. The Tang-Song narrative ends with the Mongol conquest; the Yuan-Ming transition runs in parallel with the European late medieval crisis.

While Europe was tipping into the Little Ice Age, the Mali Empire under Mansa Musa was at its imperial peak. Musa's hajj to Mecca in 1324 — across the Sahara to Cairo to Mecca and back, distributing so much gold along the way that he reportedly devalued Egyptian currency for years — was the largest royal pilgrimage of the medieval world. The Italian banking networks that would shortly finance the Renaissance were already aware of Mali's gold supply by the 1330s; the Catalan Atlas of 1375 depicts Mansa Musa holding a gold nugget.

The Norse settlements on Greenland, founded by Erik the Red around 985 CE, faded out of contact with Europe sometime between 1410 and 1500. The Little Ice Age cooling that triggered the European Great Famine was even harder on the marginal subarctic farms of Greenland, where Norse cereal agriculture had depended on Medieval Warm Period conditions that the cooling pulse simply ended.

The medieval European framework — including the Latin language used by every educated person, the Catholic Church inherited from late-antique Christianity, the city-state structure of northern Italy descended from Roman municipal law, and the persistent idea of imperial restoration — all trace back to the Roman world that ended in the West in 476. The Late Middle Ages are the period when those Roman inheritances were finally being supplemented or replaced by genuinely new institutions: the printing press, the gunpowder army, the maritime empire, the national vernacular literature.

2.blurb: The Mongol siege of the Genoese trading post at Caffa in 1346 was the conventional vector by which Yersinia pestis escaped from its central Asian endemic reservoir into the Mediterranean trade network. The disease had been spreading along the Silk Road through Mongol caravan routes from the early 1340s; the breakdown of the Pax Mongolica that had previously kept caravan traffic moving through endemic plague zones is one of the major contributing factors. The Mongol Empire's chapter 8 covers the plague and the late-empire decline from the steppe perspective.

The Ottoman beyliks rising in northwestern Anatolia in the late thirteenth and early fourteenth centuries were established as a frontier Turkish polity right when the Black Death was about to hit. The Ottoman Empire's chapter 1 covers the founding generation of Osman and Orhan; the plague's effect on Ottoman expansion is contested in the literature, but the Ottomans were certainly hit and certainly survived to consolidate the Anatolian beyliks over the next century while Europe was reeling.

While Europe was dying of the Black Death, the Polynesian voyaging society was reaching its medieval climax: Easter Island had been settled by 1200, New Zealand by 1280, and the eastern-Polynesian moai-building culture was at full development. The recent genetic evidence of South American sweet potato varieties on Polynesian islands also suggests trans-Pacific contact with the Andean kingdoms in this period — a separate strand of fourteenth-century intercontinental contact that the European-centric textbooks usually omit.

3.blurb: While England and France spent 116 years killing each other, the Ottoman Empire used the same period to consolidate the conquest of the Balkans, defeat the last great pan-European Crusade at Nicopolis in 1396 (Ottoman Empire chapter 2), and prepare the conquest of Constantinople in 1453. The European exhaustion from the Hundred Years War is one of the structural factors that allowed the Ottomans to absorb southeast Europe without serious external resistance.

The Aztec Triple Alliance was founded in 1428 — one year before Joan of Arc broke the siege of Orléans. Both the Aztec and the European late medieval world were going through their critical political-cultural founding moments in the 1420s. The Aztec founding produced the imperial system that the Spanish would conquer 90 years later, less than 30 years after Columbus.

The Inca expansion under Pachacuti began in 1438 — nine years after Joan of Arc's coronation of Charles VII at Reims. The Inca and the late-medieval European political consolidations were almost exactly contemporary; both the Inca road network and the Burgundian-Valois state-building of the same decades are early-modern political projects in essentially the same generation. The Inca would fall to Pizarro in 1532, less than a century after Pachacuti began the imperial expansion.

The Ming dynasty was founded by Zhu Yuanzhang in 1368 — right in the middle of the Hundred Years War. Zhu was a peasant-turned-warlord who overthrew the Mongol Yuan and founded the largest peasant-revolt success in world history. The Ming voyages of Zheng He from 1405 to 1433 — fleets of hundreds of ships ranging across the Indian Ocean to East Africa, with ships several times larger than the Portuguese caravels — coincided exactly with Henry V's Agincourt campaign and Joan of Arc's adolescence.

4.blurb: The papal claim to universal authority that Boniface VIII pushed to its maximum in Unam Sanctam in 1302 had developed slowly out of the late-antique bishopric of Rome, formalized by Constantine's Christianization of the Roman Empire (Ancient Rome chapter 9) and given political teeth by Gregory the Great around 600. The Boniface-Philip confrontation at Anagni was the moment the late-antique papal-imperial framework finally broke under the weight of an early-modern centralized French monarchy.

The Sufi orders that flourished across the Islamic world in the post-Mongol fourteenth and fifteenth centuries — including the Safaviyya order at Ardabil that would shortly produce the Safavid dynasty in Iran in 1501 — provide a striking parallel to the late medieval European reform movements. Both Christian and Islamic religious institutions were generating reformist popular movements outside the established hierarchies in roughly the same period. The Safavid order's chapter 1 covers the militarization of the Safaviyya in the late fourteenth and fifteenth centuries that ran in parallel with the Avignon and Schism crises.

5.blurb: The Byzantine Constantinople of the early fifteenth century — when Wycliffe's ideas were spreading through Bohemia and the Hussite Wars were starting — was a city of perhaps 35,000 living inside walls built for 400,000, with whole districts depopulated and reverting to gardens. The Byzantine reform tradition (the Hesychast controversy of the fourteenth century, the Council of Florence's failed reunion of 1438–1439) ran in parallel with the western reform movements. The Byzantine chapter 11 covers the endgame: the 1453 fall to Mehmed II that would happen 38 years after Hus was burned at Constance.

While Hus and Wycliffe were attacking the institutional Catholic Church from one direction, the Sufi orders across the Islamic world were articulating their own reformist devotional traditions. The Safaviyya order at Ardabil, which would shortly produce the Safavid dynasty in Iran, was militarizing in exactly the period the Hussite Wars were fighting their own reform-driven conflicts in Bohemia. The structural parallel — religious reform movements arming themselves and going political in the post-Mongol Eurasian system — is striking.

The Indian bhakti devotional movement of the late medieval period — Kabir, Mirabai, the early Sikh community under Guru Nanak (born 1469) — produced its own regional reform traditions that paralleled the European Wycliffite-Hussite movements. Both rejected institutional intermediaries between believer and divine, both used vernacular language to reach lay audiences, both attacked the wealth and political alignment of the established religious institutions. The medieval-India chapter 6 covers the bhakti emergence in detail.

6.blurb: The contemporary Red Turban Rebellion in China overthrew the Yuan dynasty in 1368 and produced the Ming founding under Zhu Yuanzhang — the largest peasant-revolt success in world history and the only late-medieval rural uprising to produce a long-term political revolution. The contrast with the European revolts is structural: the Chinese rebellion succeeded because the imperial system had collapsed under climate, plague, and Mongol misrule; the European rebellions failed because European royal governments, despite the Hundred Years War and the schism, had not collapsed at the same level.

7.blurb: The Council of Florence of 1438–1439 was the great failed attempt at reunion between the Latin and Greek churches before the fall of Constantinople in 1453. Cosimo de' Medici paid enormous sums to bring the Byzantine emperor John VIII Palaiologos and the Greek delegation to Italy. The reunion was repudiated by most Greeks within a few years, but the cultural exchange transformed Italian humanism: the Greek manuscripts the delegation brought west fed directly into the Italian Renaissance recovery of classical Greek literature.

The Timurid Renaissance at Herat in the late fifteenth century — Bihzad's Persian miniature painting, Jami's poetry, the brilliant illustrated manuscript tradition — was a direct contemporary of the Italian Renaissance and operated on similar patronage logic. Both the fifteenth-century Italian city-states and the fifteenth-century Timurid courts in eastern Iran were generating one of the highest-quality court cultures in human history. The Safavid dynasty that would emerge from the post-Timurid chaos in 1501 would inherit the Timurid cultural infrastructure essentially intact (Safavid Persia chapter 1).

The Italian Renaissance this chapter has been sketching gets its own full TL: the patchwork of competing city-states, the Medici cultural project, the perspectival revolution in painting, the High Renaissance triumvirate, the Venetian sea-empire, and the Counter-Reformation that closed the era. The Renaissance Italy TL is the inside view of the cultural moment whose outer crust the late-medieval European story has only touched.

8.blurb: The Theodosian Walls had repelled every besieging army since the early fifth century — the great Arab sieges of 674–678 and 717–718, the Avar siege of 626, the Bulgarian sieges of the early ninth century, even the 1204 Fourth Crusade did not breach the walls (it came over them at the harbor). The walls finally fell to gunpowder artillery in 1453. The Byzantine empire's chapter 11 covers the siege from the Greek-defensive side.

Sultan Mehmed II's conquest of Constantinople is the single defining event of the Ottoman fifteenth century and the moment the Ottoman state formally inherited the prestige and bureaucratic machinery of the Roman East. Mehmed adopted the title Kayser-i Rum (Caesar of Rome), preserved the Patriarchate within the millet system to govern his Greek Orthodox subjects, and built the Topkapi Palace as the new Ottoman imperial center. The Ottoman empire's chapter 4 covers the conquest from the Ottoman perspective.

The Roman Empire whose final fragment Mehmed II extinguished in 1453 had been founded by Romulus in 753 BCE according to the traditional date, lasted 2,206 years, and survived in continuous institutional succession through the imperial transition of Augustus (27 BCE), the Christianization under Constantine, the move of the capital to Constantinople in 330 CE, the loss of the Western half in 476, and a thousand years of medieval Greek-speaking continuation. The 1453 fall is the actual end-date of the Roman Empire — every conventional earlier date is a regional one.

The fall of Granada to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492 ended the 781-year history of Muslim political rule in Iberia that had begun with the Arab conquest of 711 CE. The Cordoban caliphate of the Islamic Golden Age (chapter 7) had been the brilliant high point; Granada was the long-tail remnant. The Reconquista's completion is the European Christian symmetric counterpart to the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople — both happened in the same forty years and shifted the religious geography of the Mediterranean for centuries afterward.

The Catholic Monarchs' completion of the Reconquista in 1492 was almost exactly contemporary with the rise of the Safaviyya order in northwestern Iran that would, in 1501, produce the Twelver Shia revolution under Ismail. Both the Spanish Reconquista and the Safavid revolution were forced religious transformations on populations that did not initially share the imposed faith — Catholicism on the Mudejares of Granada (broken by the Alhambra Decree) and Twelver Shia on the Sunni Persians. The methods and the moral problems were strikingly parallel.

The Portuguese voyages around Africa in the late fifteenth century — Bartolomeu Dias rounding the Cape in 1488, Vasco da Gama reaching India in 1498 — were the maritime endpoint of a process that had begun with European awareness of West African gold during the Mansa Musa era. The Atlantic slave trade that the Portuguese began in the mid-1400s targeted the same West African coast that supplied Mali's gold to the trans-Saharan trade. The Mali Empire's chapter 4 covers the Mansa Musa-era African economic context that the European Atlantic voyages were responding to.

The Aztec Empire would fall to Cortés in 1519–1521, less than thirty years after Columbus reached the Caribbean. The chapter 8 of Aztec Empire covers the Spanish conquest from the Aztec side: smallpox preceding Cortés, the political-religious convulsion of the eagle-on-cactus prophecy meeting the Spanish bearded gods, and the destruction of Tenochtitlan in three years.

The Inca expansion under Pachacuti began in 1438 — fifteen years before the fall of Constantinople — and the imperial system that the chapter 2 of Inca Empire covers was at full extent by Columbus's first voyage. The Inca and Aztec empires were simultaneously at their imperial peaks at the moment European maritime powers reached the Americas; both would be brought down within decades by smallpox and Spanish military technology.

The Andean political world that the Inca would shortly conquer (Inca Empire chapter 2) was the post-Wari, post-Tiwanaku landscape that the Middle Horizon Empires chapter 8 leaves at the late-Wari collapse. Several centuries of regional kingdoms — Chimú on the north coast, Lupaqa on Lake Titicaca, the Lambayeque culture — filled the gap between the Wari-Tiwanaku middle horizon and the Inca expansion that would absorb them all. None of this was visible from Europe in 1492; it would all unfold within fifty years of Columbus's first voyage.

The Andean kingdoms — Moche, Chimú, Lupaqa, Lambayeque — that filled the centuries between the Wari-Tiwanaku middle horizon and the Inca imperial expansion provide the structural counterpart to medieval European post-Roman kingdoms: regional, post-imperial, slowly building toward the next imperial consolidation. The chapter 8 of Andean Kingdoms covers the late-period political fragmentation that the Inca would shortly absorb. The 90% mortality of the indigenous population that followed Columbus would devastate all of these societies within a single century.

8.matchText: Mughal Empire would be founded in India in 1526
8.blurb: Babur's victory at Panipat in 1526 happened in the same world-historical pivot decade as Cortés at Tenochtitlan, Magellan's circumnavigation, Luther's Reformation, and Charles V's Habsburg consolidation. The Mughal founding is the South Asian regional manifestation of the same gunpowder-and-globalization revolution that ended the European Middle Ages — same technology, same kind of small-army-defeats-large-army outcome, same hinge year.
